text: Hello everyone, so today we will be talking about robot assisted Duhamel's procedure for Hirschsprung's disease which we did recently.
I was assisted by Dr. Vipul Bothra and Dr. Mohammad Takroni from Egypt who is here as a fellow in minimal access surgery with us.
It was a three year old girl child, was an already diagnosed case of Hirschsprung's disease and we had already done a diversion colostomy.
The surgical setup includes a camera port positioned just below the stoma, two working ports located in the right iliac fossa and left hypochondrium, and an assistant port in the epigastric region.
The patient is positioned with a slight head-down tilt and left-side elevation to facilitate the retraction of the small bowel away from the surgical field.
The patient is positioned in lithotomy to allow access to the anal opening for the next step after the bowel is pulled out.
The surgical team utilizes side docking with port positions that provide a panoramic view of the left side of the abdomen, including the descending colon, sigmoid colon, and pelvis.
This is the configuration we use for our laparoscopic cases as well.
So, in the beginning our protocol is that we take levelling biopsies to know what would be the level of the normal bubble.

The surgeon takes the first biopsy from the recto sigmoid junction, followed by a second biopsy from the mid sigmoid level. The third biopsy is taken from the upper sigmoid, near the junction with the descending colon.

We send them off for frozen section and by the time the frozen is assessed and we are told what level it is, we end up suturing these biopsy sites with threads because it helps us to give a marking of where it is.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the small bowel, working both distally and proximally, while awaiting the frozen biopsy report.

Now comparing it to laparoscopic, I think robotic gives you definitely much superior optics, gives you much better orientation because of the three-dimensional vision and of course the instruments are far superior.

So, once these are in place then we proceed with the surgery beyond this.

A simple trick for this is after every few minutes, just withdraw back, get your bearings as to how far you have gone.

Now the aim of our dissection here would be to avoid injury to any of those bigger vessels, whether it is superior rectal artery, whether it is in any branch of the inferior mesentery.
The surgeon carefully dissects close to the mesentery of the colon, ensuring that the bowel segment remains adequately vascularized and avoiding unnecessary devascularization.

So if you end up ligating or cutting a large vessel which supplies a much bigger area, you may end up devascularizing proximal to the extent of your dissection and that would involve needless loss of bubble length and additional dissection on your part intraoperatively.

The surgeon removes the first stitch to increase mobility in the bowel segment, preparing to apply a laparoscopic stapler at the peritoneal reflection for cutting the stump and further mobilizing the bowel.
Why we prefer using a stapler though it's a little expensive is that it keeps your dissection field clean.

There is no opening of the bowel happening, there is no spillage of intestinal contents and it goes a long way in ensuring that you don't get any infected pelvic collection later on or fever in the patient.
